title: gcd returns a silently wrong value when any operand is a bignum
goal: Found by the P6d numeric-depth sweep (2026-07-03). gcd has no bignum path: whenever AT LEAST ONE operand exceeds int64 it returns a silently wrong value. With one bignum operand it returns 1 (the bignum is effectively ignored / truncated to a coprime residue); with two bignum operands it returns INT64_MAX = 9223372036854775807. It only appears correct when the TRUE gcd happens to be 1, because the buggy path also returns 1. Examples: (gcd (* 3 (expt 2 61)) (* 5 (expt 2 61))) -> 1 (true 2^61); (gcd (expt 2 64) (expt 2 63)) -> 9223372036854775807 (true 2^63); (gcd (expt 2 64) 6) -> 1 (true 2). Corrupts any exact-arithmetic algorithm that reduces fractions or factors at bignum scale.
acceptance: tests/numeric_depth/found/gcd_bignum_wrong.esk prints OK gcd-bignum under -r and AOT
tests/numeric_depth family bignum_gcd is correct for all swept n (no baseline expected_wrong entries)
campaign_payoff: gcd is a tower primitive (lcm, rational reduction, factoring); a correct bignum gcd is required for exact rationals at scale (see ESH-0105).
found_by: scripts/run_numeric_depth.sh (family bignum_gcd, wrong from n=61 where an operand crosses int64)
